options: A processor that executes multiple instructions per cycle from a single instruction stream
A processor with multiple cores that execute different instruction streams in parallel
A processor with a very deep pipeline to increase clock frequency
A processor that uses scalar (non-vector) data types exclusively
questions.explanation: A superscalar processor issues and executes multiple instructions per cycle from a single sequential program. This is distinct from multicore (multiple instruction streams) and pipelining (single instruction per cycle stretched across many stages). Superscalar exploits instruction-level parallelism (ILP) within a program.

questions.q: In a 4-issue superscalar processor, what prevents it from sustaining 4 instructions per cycle on real workloads?
options: The CPU frequency must be reduced to support 4 parallel instructions
Instruction dependencies, limited functional units, and memory latency create bottlenecks that reduce average IPC below 4
4-issue processors are theoretically limited to 2.5 instructions per cycle
Memory caches automatically serialize instructions to prevent conflicts
questions.explanation: Real code has data dependencies, limited ALUs/multipliers, and memory stalls. A 4-issue CPU can issue 4 instructions per cycle only when 4 independent instructions are available. With typical code having ~1.5 dependencies per instruction, sustained IPC is often 1.5-2.5, not the theoretical 4.

questions.q: What is the primary advantage of superscalar execution compared to pipelining for improving single-thread performance?
options: Superscalar eliminates all instruction dependencies
Pipelining must use deeper stages; superscalar can use shallow stages and still improve throughput
Superscalar can execute multiple independent instructions in parallel instead of one per cycle, extracting instruction-level parallelism
Superscalar always has higher clock frequency than pipelined designs
questions.explanation: Pipelining achieves higher throughput via frequency scaling but still issues one instruction per cycle. Superscalar extracts true parallelism by finding multiple independent instructions to execute simultaneously. Modern CPUs combine both: deep pipelines (for frequency) and multiple issue (for IPC).

questions.q: In a superscalar processor with an issue width of 6, a typical instruction stream has an average of how many independent instructions available for parallel issue?
options: 6 independent instructions are always available
Usually 2-4 independent instructions due to data dependencies in real code
Exactly 1 instruction, since fetch must be sequential
The number depends only on cache hit rate
questions.explanation: Real programs have data dependencies (e.g., x = a + b; y = x + c). On average, ~2-4 independent instructions can be found per issue slot in typical code. Finding 6 independent instructions per cycle is rare. This is why actual IPC on a 6-issue CPU is often 3-4, not 6.

questions.q: What is instruction-level parallelism (ILP), and why is it limited in practice?
options: ILP is the number of cores in a processor; it is limited by die size
ILP is the degree to which a single program's instructions can execute in parallel; it is limited by data dependencies and control flow
ILP is identical to clock frequency; higher ILP means higher frequency
ILP has no practical limits in modern processors
questions.explanation: ILP measures independent instructions in a program. It's limited by data dependencies (x depends on y's result), control flow (branches), and memory latency. Most programs have an ILP of 3-6, making IPC >4-6 difficult even with wide superscalar hardware.

questions.q: What is a structural hazard in a superscalar processor?
options: Two instructions attempt to write to the same register in the same cycle
Multiple instructions contend for the same functional unit when it's unavailable
A branch instruction creates uncertain control flow
Register renaming fails to track dependencies correctly
questions.explanation: A structural hazard occurs when multiple instructions need the same hardware resource (e.g., two multiplies but only one multiplier). The processor must stall or defer one instruction. Data and control hazards are different categories; structural hazards are hardware-resource conflicts.

questions.q: How does register renaming improve superscalar performance?
options: It increases the processor clock frequency
It breaks false dependencies (anti-dependencies and output dependencies), allowing more instructions to issue in parallel
It eliminates the need for out-of-order execution
It reduces the physical size of the register file
questions.explanation: Register renaming maps architectural registers to physical registers, eliminating false dependencies. Without it, two writes to r1 by different instructions create a false output dependency, stalling the second write unnecessarily. Renaming allows both to execute in parallel on different physical registers.

questions.q: What is a VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word) processor, and how does it differ from a traditional superscalar?
options: VLIW processors have more cores than superscalars
VLIW statically bundles multiple operations into a single long instruction; a superscalar dynamically issues multiple instructions per cycle
VLIW is a synonym for superscalar; there is no difference
VLIW processors always have higher IPC than superscalars
questions.explanation: VLIW (Intel Itanium, TI C6x) explicitly bundles independent operations into a single instruction at compile time. Superscalars (x86, ARM, MIPS) dynamically find parallelism at runtime. VLIW simplifies hardware but requires excellent compiler support. Superscalar is more flexible but requires complex logic.

questions.q: In a superscalar with out-of-order execution, a cache miss on a load instruction blocks the entire issue stage. True or False, and why?
options: True; all superscalars must stall the pipeline on any cache miss
False; out-of-order execution allows other independent instructions to continue issuing and executing while the load is pending
False; cache misses are impossible with modern L1 caches
True; without stalling, memory coherence is violated
questions.explanation: Out-of-order superscalars don't stall on a load miss. Independent instructions continue issuing, executing, and retiring while the load waits. The reorder buffer ensures eventual in-order retirement. This is a key advantage of out-of-order over in-order superscalars.

questions.q: A 4-issue superscalar processor averages 2.5 instructions per cycle on a workload. If the processor is modified to be 8-issue with the same core clock frequency and cache hierarchy, what would you expect for average IPC?
options: Approximately 5.0 IPC (2x scaling)
Approximately 2.5 IPC (no improvement due to unchanged ILP)
Approximately 3.5-4.0 IPC (diminishing returns due to dependency limits)
Approximately 8.0 IPC (full utilization of new issue width
questions.explanation: Widening to 8-issue doesn't double IPC because the limiting factor is ILP, not hardware width. If the workload has an ILP ceiling of ~3.5-4 (typical for real code), increasing from 4-issue to 8-issue gives diminishing returns. Actual improvement is 1.4-1.6x, not 2x, because dependency patterns remain the same.
